section.title: The Siege of Samaria
section.sectionRationale: Ben-hadad besieges Samaria until famine drives its people to horrifying extremes, and the king, blaming Elisha and the LORD, vows the prophet's death. Elisha announces impossible abundance within a day, an officer scoffs, and four desperate lepers discover the LORD has routed the entire Aramean army by sound alone, leaving the camp intact for Israel's plunder. The prophecy is fulfilled to the letter, including the scoffing officer's own death.
section.thesis: When the LORD's people are reduced to unthinkable horror by siege and famine, the LORD alone can turn desperate scarcity into sudden abundance overnight, and His spoken word through His prophet is fulfilled exactly, both in blessing for the humble and in judgment for the one who doubted.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the siege, famine, and the king's fury against Elisha, 6:24 to 33; Elisha's prophecy of plenty and the officer's scoffing, 7:1 to 2; the lepers discover the abandoned camp, 7:3 to 15; and the prophecy fulfilled to the letter, 7:16 to 20.

units.label: Siege, famine, and the king's fury
units.note: we boiled my son and ate him

verses.text: Some time later, Ben-hadad king of Aram assembled his entire army and marched up to besiege Samaria.

So there was a great {{raab|famine}} in Samaria. Indeed, they besieged the city so long that a donkey's head sold for eighty shekels of silver, and a quarter cab of dove's dung sold for five shekels of silver.
As the king of Israel was passing by on the wall, a woman cried out to him, "Help me, my lord the king!"
He answered, "If the LORD does not help you, where can I find help for you? From the threshing floor or the winepress?"
Then the king asked her, "What is the matter?" And she answered, "This woman said to me, 'Give up your son, that we may eat him, and tomorrow we will eat my son.'
So we boiled my son and ate him, and the next day I said to her, 'Give up your son, that we may eat him.' But she had hidden her son."
When the king heard the words of the woman, he tore his clothes. And as he passed by on the wall, the people saw the sackcloth under his clothes next to his skin.
He announced, "May God punish me, and ever so severely, if the head of Elisha son of Shaphat remains on his shoulders through this day!"

Now Elisha was sitting in his house, and the elders were sitting with him. The king sent a messenger ahead, but before he arrived, Elisha said to the elders, "Do you see how this murderer has sent someone to cut off my head? Look, when the messenger comes, shut the door to keep him out. Is not the sound of his master's footsteps behind him?"
While Elisha was still speaking with them, the messenger came down to him. And the king said, "This calamity is from the LORD. Why should I wait for the LORD any longer?"

units.label: Elisha's prophecy and the officer's scorn
units.note: you will see it with your own eyes, but you will not eat any of it

verses.text: Then Elisha said, "Hear the word of the LORD! This is what the LORD says: 'About this time tomorrow at the gate of Samaria, a {{seah|seah}} of fine flour will sell for a shekel, and two seahs of barley will sell for a shekel.'"

But the officer on whose arm the king leaned answered the man of God, "Look, even if the LORD were to make windows in heaven, could this really happen?" "You will see it with your own eyes," replied Elisha, "but you will not eat any of it."

units.label: The lepers discover the abandoned camp
units.note: today is a day of good news

verses.text: Now there were four men with leprosy at the entrance of the city gate, and they said to one another, "Why just sit here until we die?

If we say, 'Let us go into the city,' we will die there from the famine in the city; but if we sit here, we will also die. So come now, let us go over to the camp of the Arameans. If they let us live, we will live; if they kill us, we will die."
So they arose at twilight and went to the camp of the Arameans. But when they came to the outskirts of the camp, there was not a man to be found.

For the Lord had caused the Arameans to hear the {{qol|sound}} of chariots, horses, and a great army, so that they said to one another, "Look, the king of Israel must have hired the kings of the Hittites and Egyptians to attack us."
Thus the Arameans had arisen and fled at twilight, abandoning their tents and horses and donkeys. The camp was intact, and they had run for their lives.

When the lepers reached the edge of the camp, they went into a tent to eat and drink. Then they carried off the silver, gold, and clothing, and went and hid them. On returning, they entered another tent, carried off some items from there, and hid them.

Finally, they said to one another, "We are not doing what is right. Today is a day of {{besorah|good news}}. If we are silent and wait until morning light, our sin will overtake us. Now, therefore, let us go and tell the king's household."
So they went and called out to the gatekeepers of the city, saying, "We went to the Aramean camp and no one was there, not a trace, only tethered horses and donkeys, and the tents were intact."
The gatekeepers shouted the news, and it was reported to the king's household.

So the king got up in the night and said to his servants, "Let me tell you what the Arameans have done to us. They know we are starving, so they have left the camp to hide in the field, thinking, 'When they come out of the city, we will take them alive and enter the city.'"

But one of his servants replied, "Please, have scouts take five of the horses that remain in the city. Their plight will be no worse than all the Israelites who are left here. You can see that all the Israelites here are doomed. So let us send them and find out."
Then the scouts took two chariots with horses, and the king sent them after the Aramean army, saying, "Go and see."
And they tracked them as far as the Jordan, and indeed, the whole way was littered with the clothing and equipment the Arameans had thrown off in haste. So the scouts returned and told the king.

units.label: The prophecy fulfilled to the letter
units.note: just as the man of God had told the king

verses.text: Then the people went out and plundered the camp of the Arameans. It was then that a seah of fine flour sold for a shekel, and two seahs of barley sold for a shekel, according to the word of the LORD.

Now the king had appointed the officer on whose arm he leaned to be in charge of the gate, but the people trampled him in the gateway, and he died, just as the man of God had foretold when the king had come to him.
It happened just as the man of God had told the king: "About this time tomorrow at the gate of Samaria, two seahs of barley will sell for a shekel, and a seah of fine flour will sell for a shekel."

And the officer had answered the man of God, "Look, even if the LORD were to make windows in heaven, could this really happen?" So Elisha had replied, "You will see it with your own eyes, but you will not eat any of it!"
And that is just what happened to him. The people trampled him in the gateway, and he died.

raab.gloss: Famine. The siege drives Samaria's famine to such extremes that a donkey's head and even dove's dung become traded commodities, and two mothers resort to devouring their own sons, fulfilling the horrifying covenant warnings of Deuteronomy 28:53-57 against persistent unfaithfulness.

seah.gloss: A dry measure of roughly 7.3 liters. Elisha's prophecy that a seah of fine flour would sell for a mere shekel the very next day, after famine prices had spiraled to nearly unthinkable extremes, promised a reversal so drastic that the king's own officer could not believe it.

qol.gloss: Sound or voice. The LORD caused the Arameans to hear a qol of chariots, horses, and a great army, though no such army was actually present, routing an entire besieging force through sound alone and proving that He needs no visible weapon to accomplish deliverance.

besorah.gloss: Good news or glad tidings, the same word family later used for gospel, the good news of Christ. The lepers' insistence that they must not keep this besorah to themselves models the instinct that good news, once received, demands to be shared rather than hoarded.

basic.context: Ben-hadad's siege reduces Samaria to unthinkable famine, driving two women to an act of cannibalism that horrifies even the king, who blames Elisha and the LORD and vows to kill the prophet. Elisha, unmoved, announces that within a day prices will collapse from famine extremes to ordinary abundance, a promise so unbelievable that the king's own officer mocks it. That very night, unseen by anyone, the LORD causes the besieging Aramean army to hear the sound of a massive approaching force and flee in panic, abandoning a fully stocked camp. Four desperate lepers, expecting only death, discover the empty camp, feast, then responsibly report the good news, leading Israel to plunder unimaginable abundance exactly as Elisha foretold, down to the scoffing officer's own death.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing a specific siege, famine, and deliverance; it is not a promise that every crisis will resolve overnight or that skeptics will always meet a literal, immediate downfall. What endures is the LORD's power to reverse the most desperate circumstances instantly and completely, and the certainty that His spoken word, whether of blessing or of warning, comes to pass exactly as declared.
basic.originalLanguages: The raab, famine, described here fulfills the terrifying covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28, yet even here Elisha promises reversal: a seah of flour, an ordinary measure, restored to ordinary prices within a day. The LORD accomplishes deliverance merely by causing the enemy to hear a qol, sound, of a phantom army, no battle required. The lepers cannot keep the besorah, good news, to themselves, an instinct the gospel itself will later require of all who receive it.
basic.typology: Deliverance accomplished entirely by the LORD without a single Israelite sword raised anticipates the pattern of salvation by grace, won by God's own action rather than human effort, discovered first by society's outcasts, the lepers, who then cannot help but share the good news with others.
basic.christ: The lepers, themselves ritually excluded and expecting nothing but death, are the first to discover and receive the sudden abundance, and their insistence on sharing the good news anticipates how the gospel of Christ is first received by the humble and outcast, who then cannot keep silent, Luke 4:18, 1 Corinthians 1:26-29. The unbelieving officer who sees the miracle yet is trampled before tasting it pictures the danger of witnessing God's grace without ever partaking of it by faith.
basic.god: God can turn the most desperate scarcity into sudden abundance without any human effort, showing His power is not limited by circumstances however extreme. He also holds His word utterly reliable, both to bless the humble sufferers and to bring exactly the judgment He speaks upon willful unbelief, refusing to let either promise fall to the ground unfulfilled.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 28:53-57 foretold the horrifying famine cannibalism now literally fulfilled in Samaria's siege, the covenant curse for persistent unfaithfulness. Isaiah 37:36 records another besieging army routed by the LORD without a battle, the same pattern of deliverance without human combat. Hebrews 3:12-19 warns against the unbelief the officer displayed, seeing but never truly trusting.
basic.oneStory: From the depths of covenant judgment, cannibalism under siege, the LORD brings sudden, total deliverance discovered first by society's outcasts, who cannot keep the news to themselves, while the one who saw but never believed is destroyed exactly as warned, a pattern that anticipates the gospel: grace received by the humble, judgment falling on unbelief that witnesses but never trusts.

context.literary: The narrative deliberately juxtaposes the horror of 6:24-33 with the promise of 7:1-2, then withholds the reader's knowledge of what actually happened to the Aramean camp until the lepers discover it, building suspense before the double confirmation, first by the lepers' report, then by the king's own scouts, that the promised reversal has truly occurred, closing with the scoffing officer's fate narrated twice for emphasis.
context.historical: Samaria, Israel's capital, was a fortified hill city difficult to starve out quickly, making an extended siege plausible; the famine prices described, a donkey's head and dove's dung as food, reflect the kind of extreme scarcity documented in other ancient siege accounts. Ancient armies were known to panic and flee based on perceived threats, and this account attributes such a rout directly to divine intervention rather than any human agency.
context.canonical: This siege fulfills, almost verbatim, the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:53-57 concerning cannibalism under siege, showing Israel's persistent unfaithfulness bringing exactly the judgment Moses warned of centuries earlier, while the deliverance anticipates a similar rout of Assyrian forces around Jerusalem in 2 Kings 19.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the LORD can reverse even the most extreme, covenant-curse level suffering instantly and completely through His spoken word, and that His word is certain both to bless the desperate and to judge willful, mocking unbelief exactly as declared.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes one specific historical siege, famine, and deliverance; it does not prescribe a formula for when or how God will end suffering, nor does it teach that all skeptics face literal trampling. What endures is confidence in God's power to reverse desperate circumstances and the seriousness of unbelief that witnesses grace without receiving it.

debates.question: Why did the king blame Elisha and the LORD for the famine rather than Ben-hadad's siege, 6:31, 33?

views.label: Misdirected anger reflecting the king's fundamentally faithless heart
views.case: The king's fury at Elisha, despite Elisha having repeatedly protected Israel, 6:9-10, exposes a deeper unbelief already present in Israel's leadership, blaming God's messenger rather than repenting of the persistent idolatry that brought covenant judgment in the first place, consistent with the pattern throughout Kings of blaming prophets for bad news rather than heeding their warnings.

views.label: Genuine despair after years of compounding national crisis
views.case: By this point Israel had suffered ongoing Aramean aggression for years, chapters 5 to 6, and the king's exasperated cry, why should I wait for the LORD any longer, may reflect the raw despair of a genuinely desperate man rather than settled theological rebellion, a despair Elisha's prophecy immediately and graciously answers rather than condemns.

debates.bearing: Either way, Elisha's calm response and immediate prophecy of deliverance shows that the LORD's mercy was not withdrawn even from a faithless king and a despairing nation, answering unbelief with abundant, undeserved grace.

study.originalLanguages: The raab, famine, fulfills almost word for word the ancient covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28, yet the LORD promises reversal to an ordinary seah of flour at ordinary prices within a single day. He accomplishes total deliverance by nothing more than a qol, sound, causing an entire army to flee a threat that did not exist. And the lepers, who might have kept their sudden windfall secret, instead insist this is a day of besorah, good news, that cannot be hoarded but must be shared with the whole suffering city.

typology.type: Total deliverance achieved without a single blow struck by Israel
typology.fulfillment: Salvation accomplished entirely by God's own action through Christ, apart from human works, Ephesians 2:8-9.

typology.type: Outcasts, the lepers, first discovering and unable to withhold the good news
typology.fulfillment: The gospel first received by the humble and outcast, who then compellingly proclaim it to others, Luke 4:18, 1 Corinthians 1:27-29.

study.christ: The deliverance of Samaria comes entirely by the LORD's own unseen action, no Israelite sword is raised, foreshadowing salvation accomplished by Christ's finished work alone, received by faith rather than earned by human effort, Ephesians 2:8-9. That the outcast lepers are the first to discover this abundant good news, and cannot keep it to themselves, anticipates how the gospel is first embraced by society's overlooked and humble, who then become its most urgent messengers, Luke 4:18. The unbelieving officer, permitted to see the miracle yet trampled before tasting its benefit, warns that beholding grace is not the same as receiving it by faith, a sober picture of those who witness the gospel yet never truly believe, Hebrews 3:12-19.
study.god: God can reverse the most extreme, covenant-curse level suffering completely and instantly, needing no human effort or weapon to accomplish deliverance. He is also unshakably faithful to His spoken word, fulfilling both the promise of abundance for a desperate, humbled people and the precise warning of judgment against one officer's scoffing unbelief, showing that His word never returns void in either direction.

crossReferences.note: Foretells the exact horror of siege-induced cannibalism now literally fulfilled in Samaria, the covenant curse for persistent unfaithfulness.
Records another besieging army, Sennacherib's Assyrians, routed by the LORD without any human battle, the same pattern of deliverance seen here.
Warns against the kind of unbelief the scoffing officer displayed, seeing God's works yet failing to truly trust and benefit from them.
Salvation as entirely God's gracious accomplishment, not human achievement, the pattern this deliverance foreshadows.

study.oneStory: From the depths of a covenant-curse famine, God brings total, sudden deliverance discovered first by desperate outcasts who cannot keep such good news to themselves, while the one man who saw the miracle but scoffed in unbelief is destroyed exactly as warned. This whole sequence anticipates the shape of the gospel: grace poured out abundantly on the humble and undeserving, received by faith alone, while unbelief that merely observes without trusting meets the very judgment it doubted.

quiz.q: What horrifying extremity did the famine in besieged Samaria drive two women to commit?

options.text: They agreed to boil and eat their own sons in successive days, though the second woman hid her son to avoid her part of the agreement.
options.note: Verses 28 to 29 record this exact horrifying account brought before the king.

options.text: They fled the city together to surrender to the Arameans.
options.note: The text describes a horrifying act of cannibalism within the city, not an escape or surrender.

options.text: They simply requested more grain rations from the king.
options.note: Their appeal to the king concerned this act of cannibalism specifically, a far graver matter than rationing.

options.text: Nothing unusual happened; the city had ample food.
options.note: The text explicitly describes an extreme famine and horrifying acts resulting from it.

quiz.q: What did Elisha prophesy would happen at the gate of Samaria by the next day, and how did the king's officer respond?

options.text: He prophesied famine prices would collapse to ordinary levels within a day, and the officer scoffed that this was impossible even if the LORD made windows in heaven.
options.note: 7:1-2 records both the prophecy and the officer's explicit disbelief.

options.text: He prophesied the siege would continue for another year.
options.note: Elisha prophesied an imminent, drastic reversal within about a day, not continued suffering.

options.text: The officer believed immediately and thanked Elisha.
options.note: The officer explicitly mocked the prophecy as impossible rather than believing it.

options.text: Elisha refused to give any specific prophecy.
options.note: Elisha gave a very specific, dated prophecy about prices at the city gate.

quiz.q: How did the Aramean army come to abandon its camp, and who discovered it first?

options.text: The LORD caused them to hear the sound of a great approaching army and flee in panic, and four lepers, going to surrender or die, discovered the abandoned camp first.
options.note: 7:6-8 records the cause of the flight and the lepers' discovery.

options.text: Israel's army attacked and drove the Arameans out by force.
options.note: No battle occurred; the Arameans fled based on a sound the LORD caused them to hear, without any actual attack.

options.text: The king of Israel personally led a surprise raid on the camp.
options.note: The king was unaware of the abandonment until informed by the lepers' report through the gatekeepers.

options.text: The Arameans never left their camp.
options.note: The text explicitly states they fled at twilight, abandoning tents, horses, and donkeys.

quiz.q: Why did the four lepers decide to report their discovery rather than keep it secret?

options.text: They concluded they were not doing right by staying silent on a day of good news, fearing that delay would bring judgment on them, so they told the king's household.
options.note: Verse 9 records their exact reasoning and resulting decision to report the news.

options.text: They were caught by Aramean soldiers who forced them to confess.
options.note: No soldiers remained to catch them; their decision to report came from their own conscience.

options.text: They wanted to keep all the plunder for themselves permanently.
options.note: Their stated motive was conscience about withholding good news, not securing plunder exclusively for themselves.

options.text: They never reported anything, and the city remained unaware.
options.note: The text explicitly records them reporting to the gatekeepers, who informed the king's household.

quiz.q: How was Elisha's prophecy about prices and about the skeptical officer both fulfilled?

options.text: Prices collapsed to a seah of flour for a shekel exactly as foretold, and the officer, appointed to oversee the gate, was trampled to death by the crowd, seeing the abundance but never eating any of it.
options.note: 7:16-20 records both fulfillments in careful, repeated detail.

options.text: Prices remained high despite Elisha's prophecy.
options.note: The text explicitly states prices fell exactly as Elisha had foretold.

options.text: The officer survived and enjoyed the abundance himself.
options.note: The officer was trampled to death and explicitly did not eat any of the food, precisely as prophesied.

options.text: Neither part of the prophecy came true.
options.note: Both the pricing collapse and the officer's death are recorded as exact fulfillments of Elisha's words.

questions: Samaria's siege reduced people to unthinkable horror, yet the LORD's deliverance came suddenly and completely. What situation in your life feels too far gone for God to turn around, and how does this account challenge that assumption?

The officer saw the very door to belief opened by Elisha's clear prophecy, yet mocked it, and it cost him everything. Where might skepticism be keeping you from receiving a promise or provision God has clearly set before you?

The lepers, the very people society had cast out, were the first to discover and enjoy God's abundant provision. How does it change your view of yourself, or others, to know God often reveals His grace first to the overlooked and excluded?

The lepers felt they could not keep the good news to themselves, even though silence would have benefited them personally. What good news has God given you that you are tempted to keep to yourself rather than share?

The LORD delivered Samaria with no human battle at all, defeating an army with sound alone. Where do you need to trust God to work in a situation where you feel completely powerless to change anything yourself?

translationNotes.note: The famine prices recorded, a donkey's head for eighty shekels and a small measure of dove's dung for five shekels, reflect the collapse of ordinary food supply to the point that normally inedible or unclean items became traded commodities.

A seah, roughly 7.3 liters, of fine flour selling for a single shekel represents an extraordinary return to ordinary, even generous, pre-famine pricing, making Elisha's prophecy strikingly specific and easily falsifiable if untrue.

The Arameans' assumption that Israel had hired the kings of the Hittites and Egyptians reflects real geopolitical anxieties of the era, showing how a supernatural sound was interpreted through entirely natural, plausible fears.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 6:24-7:20. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this passage.
